purpose: Hard-topic Council responses used to keep transparency views stable when providers disagree, fail, or return sparse evidence.

fixtures.title: Council fixture: heavy disagreement
fixtures.question: How should Romans 9 be weighed in debates about election?
fixtures.weakness: Positions disagree heavily and the UI must explain why one argument ranked higher.

positions.label: Corporate election emphasis
positions.summary: Romans 9 can be read as emphasizing covenant history and corporate identity before individual salvation mechanics.

evidence.quote: For they are not all Israel, which are of Israel.
evidence.reasoning: The argument turns on the identity of Israel within the covenant line.

positions.why_not_higher: It must still account for individual-sounding mercy language.
positions.confidence_rationale: The position has contextual support but faces direct challenge from nearby verses.

positions.label: Individual election emphasis
positions.summary: Romans 9 can be read as teaching God's freedom in individual election and mercy.

evidence.quote: I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy.
evidence.reasoning: The verse gives the strongest direct support for an individual-election reading.

positions.why_not_higher: It can understate the chapter's covenant-history frame.
positions.confidence_rationale: Strong textual support remains contested by context.

synthesis.dissent_notes: Both positions retain plausible readings; the fixture should never hide the near tie.
unresolved_tensions: Corporate context and individual mercy language both remain visible.
synthesis.synthesis: The Council narrowly ranks the corporate emphasis higher because it explains the chapter's Israel frame while preserving the force of the mercy text.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: The leading position wins narrowly, with direct evidence on both sides.

evidence_classification.reasoning: Primary evidence for the leading position.
Strong challenge from the alternative position.

retrieved_evidence.text: Not as though the word of God hath taken none effect. For they are not all Israel, which are of Israel:
For he saith to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy...

fixtures.title: Council fixture: provider failure
fixtures.question: How should James 2 and Romans 4 be compared?
fixtures.weakness: One provider fails and the confidence view must surface the failure without breaking the explanation.

positions.label: Different questions, compatible answers
positions.summary: Romans 4 addresses justification apart from works of law; James 2 addresses a dead claim of faith without works.

evidence.quote: his faith is counted for righteousness
evidence.reasoning: Romans emphasizes faith apart from works.

evidence.quote: faith, if it hath not works, is dead
evidence.reasoning: James challenges empty profession rather than living faith.

positions.why_not_higher: The synthesis depends on careful distinction between uses of works and justify.
positions.confidence_rationale: Evidence is strong, but one provider failed.

positions.label: Tension remains unresolved
positions.summary: The texts use overlapping language, so the tension should remain visible.
positions.why_not_higher: It explains difficulty but not the different argumentative settings as well.

synthesis.dissent_notes: Provider failure should lower confidence and remain visible in the audit trail.
unresolved_tensions: The word justify is used in distinguishable but overlapping contexts.
synthesis.synthesis: The compatible-reading position ranks higher because it accounts for both passages' stated targets.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: Direct evidence supports the synthesis, but provider failure reduces confidence.

positions.label: Different questions, compatible answers
positions.summary: The passages can be harmonized contextually.

voices.error: Fixture provider timeout while testing failure rendering.

retrieved_evidence.text: But to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly...
Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone.

fixtures.title: Council fixture: sparse evidence
fixtures.question: What can be said about a topic when retrieval returns little direct evidence?
fixtures.weakness: Sparse evidence should produce cautious explanations instead of overconfident claims.

positions.label: Cautious answer
positions.summary: The Council should say what the limited evidence supports and name what remains uncertain.
positions.confidence_rationale: Confidence stays low because the fixture intentionally lacks evidence.

synthesis.dissent_notes: Sparse retrieval should be visible rather than silently filled with confident prose.
unresolved_tensions: No direct evidence is available in this fixture.
synthesis.synthesis: A cautious answer is the only defensible output when supporting evidence is sparse.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: Sparse evidence and one voice only.
